data.title: June FOMC Meeting: Ignore The Dot Plot In This Uncertain Environment
data.description:
By Seema Shah, Chief Global Strategist; Christian Floro, CFA, CMT, Market Strategist; and Magdalena Ocampo, Market Strategist

At its June meeting, the Federal Open Market Committee decided to keep its benchmark policy rate unchanged at 4.25%-4.50% as expected.

The new Summary of Economic Projections (SEP) showed a weaker economy in 2025, a marginal increase in the unemployment rate, and a higher inflation forecast, while the median dot plot remained unchanged at two rate cuts for this year.

Yet, with Fed Chair Powell emphasizing the elevated state of uncertainty around their policy outlook (“With uncertainty as elevated as it is, no one holds these rate paths with a lot of conviction”), he essentially de-emphasized the dot plot.

The key takeaway from the June meeting came not from the SEP, but from Powell’s remark during the press conference: “For the time being, we are well-positioned to wait to learn more about the likely course of the economy before considering any adjustments to our policy stance.” In essence, the continued strength of the U.S. economy gives the Fed room to pause and wait for clearer data before making any policy changes.

Current assessment

The U.S. economy has defied recession fears, with hard data cooling only slightly in recent months. The labor market is showing only modest signs of cracks, while inflation has surprised on the downside of late.

Recent surveys suggest that firms are likely to pass through some or all of their tariff-induced costs, so an inflation boost is increasingly likely in Q3. Tariff uncertainty has likely peaked, but it has been replaced by growing fiscal and geopolitical uncertainty – creating a difficult environment for forecasters.

From Fed Chair Powell’s perspective, the continued robustness of the economy suggests that the current policy stance is only modestly restrictive, in turn indicating only a shallow cutting cycle is likely over the forecast horizon.

He noted that while inflation is likely to move higher over the coming months as tariffs are passed along the supply chain, it is highly unclear if the tariff-driven inflation will be short-lived or persistent.

Certainly, the risk that unanchored inflation expectations - even in the short term - leads to persistent inflation is heightened, especially in an environment where overall price stability has not yet been achieved. Against that backdrop, the Fed has little reason to cut rates preemptively.

Updates to the Summary of Economic Projections

The new Summary of Economic Projections was also released on Wednesday. In general, it showed the Fed moving further away from both parts of its dual mandate, underscoring the difficult dilemma the central bank currently faces.

  • The GDP growth forecast for 2025 was revised down from 1.7% to 1.4%, from 1.8% to 1.6% in 2026, and was left unchanged at 1.8% for 2027.
  • The unemployment rate forecast was revised up slightly, from 4.4% to 4.5% in 2025, and remaining there in 2026.
  • The headline PCE inflation forecast for 2025 was revised up, from 2.7% to 3%. With headline inflation currently sitting at just 2.1%, the Fed is projecting a sharp increase in inflation over the coming months. Importantly, though, inflation is then projected to fall back to 2.4% in 2026, suggesting that the Fed expects the tariff impact on inflation to be fairly short-lived (transitory!)

The revised SEP forecasts for 2025 are very similar to our own. We expect GDP growth this year of 1.5%, inflation to reach 3.2%, and unemployment to rise to 4.5%.

Despite the changes to the inflation forecasts, there was no change to the 2025 median dot plot, which still shows two cuts this year. The plot was finely split: 10 participants projected two or more cuts in 2025, and 9 participants projected one cut or no cuts.

Overall, there was a slight hawkish shift in the distribution of dots for this year, with 7 of 19 participants now seeing no cuts this year, up from 4 participants in March.

The median dot now sees just one cut in 2026, down from two cuts in the March SEP, and one more cut in 2027, ultimately taking the Fed funds rate down to 3.375%. The median longer-run dot was unchanged at 3%.

FOMC dot projections

June 2025

Source: Federal Reserve, Clearnomics, Principal Asset Management. Data as of June 18, 2025.

Policy outlook

The unchanged median dot showing 50 bps of cuts for this year despite the higher inflation outlook is somewhat surprising. Yet, any change in this year’s dot plot may have been interpreted as a signal that the Fed has a clear understanding about its future policy path.

As Powell clearly conveyed throughout the press conference, with the economic outlook still very much shrouded in uncertainty, the Fed is still unsure of how things will pan out.

With inflation already running above target and economic data still looking resilient, the current environment of persistent uncertainty is ripe ground for a policy misstep. The wisest decision for the Fed is to simply stand still – both on its current policy rates and its dot plot. Our view is that the Fed will remain on hold until late Q4, with just one 25 bp cut this year.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Oracle's Multi-Cloud Strategy Shift Is Proving To Be A Success
data.description:
Oracle Corporation (NYSE:ORCL)(NEOE:ORAC:CA) is beginning to be recognized as a major cloud player amongst its larger hyperscaler peers. With RPO expected to double in eFY26, Oracle is accelerating the development of its data center capacity with a $25b capital outlay for the fiscal year. With enterprises increasing the adoption of GenAI and agentic AI, Oracle should realize substantial consumption growth across its cloud platform, driving top-line growth in eFY26.

Despite the substantial growth outlay, power capacity remains constrained and may push out or delay some large-scale projects.

Going into a position, I believe investors should be cautious about the premium, which currently sits at a relative high at 22.40x EV/aEBITDA on a trailing twelve-month basis. I believe investors should be patient and wait for weakness in the share price before initiating or building a position. I personally reduced my position following the q4’25 earnings release and will be looking for a new entry point. My suggested reentry point would be $191/share at the midpoint average of ORCL shares' historical trading premium at 20.54x EV/aEBITDA.

Placing a value on the firm, I believe ORCL shares should be priced at $271/share at 19.69x eFY28 EV/aEBITDA. I am recommending ORCL shares with a STRONG BUY.

Oracle Corporation Operations

Oracle reported $138b in remaining performance obligations in q4’25, a 41% year-over-year increase, driven by significant demand for Oracle Cloud Infrastructure [OCI]. As management reiterated in the q4’25 earnings call, “demand continues to dramatically outstrip supply.” Management is anticipating OCI to grow by over 70% in eFY26, a major acceleration from the 50% year-over-year growth rate realized in FY25 results. With demand for cloud services remaining strong, management is anticipating RPO to expand by 100% in eFY26.

One of the driving growth factors will be Oracle Autonomous Database. This is drawn upon the increase in data produced with the emergence of GenAI. For instance, when ChatGPT was released in 2022, data created reached 101 zettabytes, 50x the amount generated 12 years prior. It is estimated that 552 zettabytes will be generated between 2024-2026 as a result of the mass adoption of AI applications. I suspect that this figure may be light, given that only 78% of organizations have adopted some form of AI technologies. As agentic AI is further adopted, I believe data generation will substantially accelerate, driving the need for storage capacity. In q4’24, CyberArk (CYBR) reported that there were 82 machine identities for every one human in organizations worldwide. With the assumption that only 78% of organizations are currently leveraging AI, I suspect there will be significant scaling over the next year, potentially overshooting estimates by a wide margin.

Enterprise AI adoption is expected to significantly drive consumption for Oracle as customer-specific data is utilized in AI applications and large language models [LLMs]. Having the capability of developing and deploying AI agents in one ecosystem should drive significant growth.

Oracle also realized substantial growth from its MultiCloud database strategy, growing 115% sequentially in q4’25. This component of the business involves partnership across the major hyperscalers, including Amazon (AMZN) AWS, Alphabet (GOOG) Google Cloud, and Microsoft (MSFT) Azure. As part of this integrated partnership, Oracle has 23 data center locations; additionally, it has 47 in development over the next 12 months. Management anticipates momentum in the MultiCloud business to continue in eFY26, growing at a triple-digit percentage rate.

In addition to this, Oracle Cloud@Customer has grown substantially, with 29 dedicated data centers live and another 30 being built in eFY26. Oracle Cloud@Customer is essentially a managed private cloud data center completely outfitted with OCI. A few of the benefits of Cloud@Customer is that it provides organizations with the same autonomous capabilities in the database while providing security and governance.

This could potentially drive revenue in Oracle’s IaaS platform, which realized a 59% year-over-year increase in consumption revenue in FY25.

Oracle Corporation Financial Position

Looking to eq1’26, I’m forecasting Oracle to generate $15b in net revenue, with an adjusted EPS of $1.48/share. I believe growth will primarily be driven by strong cloud migrations in the coming fiscal year as more data center capacity comes online. Though management indicated that the Project Stargate hasn’t impacted RPO, I suspect there should be a ramp-up in the coming years as power capacity remains a headwind for the Texas location.

Oracle reported a -$394mm cash outflow for FY25, primarily the result of significant investments in data center equipment. Capital investments for FY25 increased by over 2x as a result of these large investments. Despite the cash outflow experienced in the fiscal year, these investments should cycle through to the top line in the coming fiscal year, driving significant revenue growth for the organization. Looking to eFY26, management guided a capital outlay of $25b+ to close the gap between capacity and demand. This could potentially place Oracle in a consecutive fiscal year of cash outflow.

Risks Related to Oracle Corporation

Bull Case

Oracle is building out significant data center capacity eFY26, providing a major ramp-up for cloud revenue growth. With enterprises turning to Oracle Cloud Database for autonomous database operations, I believe Oracle will realize substantial growth in consumption, particularly as more processes are automated with agentic AI.

Bear Case

ORCL shares are trading at an excessive premium to their hyperscaler peers, potentially adding significant near-term risk to gyration or revaluation. Power capacity continues to be a constraint for large data center build-outs, which can be confirmed by organizations like GE Vernova (GEV) and Siemens Energy (OTCPK:SMNEY), both of which have gas turbine manufacturing backlogs extending beyond 2028. This could potentially delay large build-outs and push out revenue growth for Oracle.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

On an unadjusted basis, ORCL shares have turned to trading at a significant premium above its peer hyperscalers at 27.76x EV/EBITDA.

Despite the high premium, I believe the valuation is justified given Oracle’s significant growth outlay across enterprise customers. With more workloads migrating to the cloud to enable autonomous database operations, I believe Oracle’s growth may continue to remain strong for the coming years.

Given the high premium and recent run-up in valuation, I suspect shares may experience some gyration in the near-term, potentially opening up a better opportunity for building one’s position. I recently reduced my ORCL position with this in mind. My suggested reentry point would be $191/share at the midpoint average of ORCL shares' historical trading premium at 20.54x EV/aEBITDA.

Placing a value on the firm, I believe ORCL shares should be priced at $271/share at 19.69x eFY28 EV/aEBITDA. I am recommending ORCL shares with a STRONG BUY.

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle, with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market cap and relative multiple.

data.title: Gold Royalty: Cash Flow Turning, But Still Not Investable
data.description:
Gold Royalty Corp. (NYSE:GROY) is still in that early phase where it's trying to move from story to something that actually brings in money. The idea is simple, they collect royalties from other people's mines, no capex, no operations. Sounds nice, but in reality it's been kind of frozen for a while. Only seven assets are producing, cash is low, and the debt includes convertibles, so not much space for things to go wrong. The portfolio is growing, that's true, and Q1 2025 was the first real sign that cash flow can turn. Some big mines like Odyssey, Côté, and Vareš are slowly getting closer to full speed. But I still don't see this as something to buy. Not yet. It's a HOLD for now.

Q1 2025 Results

Gold Royalty pulled in $3.14 million in revenue, up from $2.89 million in Q1 2024, so a small step forward. That growth mostly came from Borborema kicking in for the first time and some steady contributions from Cozamin and Malartic. Nothing explosive, but the trend is at least pointed in the right direction.

But there's one thing to note, GEOs actually dropped from 2,019 in Q1 last year to 1,249 now. So even though revenue is up, the ounces backing that revenue aren't, and this can hurt in the long run. This has more to do with the mix and timing of receipts than declining performance, but it's worth watching. Not all royalties hit every quarter the same way, and it might be dangerous to invest.

Net loss came in at $1.25 million, which is still red, but slightly better than the $1.40 million loss last year. They also managed to bring G&A down to $1.8 million from $2.3 million. That cut alone explains part of the smaller loss. No need to overanalyze here, they're spending less and it helps.

Cash flow from operations turned positive, $2.49 million this quarter, vs. just $340k last year. That's actually meaningful. The company is starting to generate cash from the producing royalties without needing to raise or borrow more, something that wasn't happening in 2024. Adjusted EBITDA hit $1.66 million, which isn't game-changing, but it's the first sign this company is starting to support itself.

Cash on hand sits at $3.21 million, while debt remains big: $26.1 million bank loan, $25.5 million in convertibles. They did extend their credit facility during the quarter, up to $30 million, and stretched it to 2028, which gives them some breathing room, but it doesn't fix the capital structure. This company still runs tight. Bottom line, it's better than Q1 last year. Still small, still fragile, but doing better. Some royalties are now producing, others are getting close. If gold holds, this model starts to work. But it's early. No high-fives yet.

Risks

Gold Royalty is still early. It's got a big royalty portfolio, but only a small part of it is actually generating cash. Right now, just seven assets are producing, and the rest are somewhere between exploration and construction. That means the company is still dependent on future production, and future usually means delays, cost overruns, or partners running into problems. If a few big projects slip, like Odyssey, Vareš, or Borborema, the expected cash flow growth just doesn't happen.

Then there's the debt. About $51 million is split between a credit facility and convertibles. That's not a huge number in the royalty world, but for a company with just $3 million in cash and still posting net losses, it's not comfortable either. They've got the credit line extended, which helps, but if metal prices drop or one of the key assets underdelivers, things could tighten fast.

The whole model is also tied to the gold price, obviously. GROY isn't operating mines, it's taking a cut of revenue, so if gold drops back below $2,000, some of these assets don't generate much. Same with copper. The Vareš stream looks great right now with copper around $4.80/lb, but if that turns south, the contribution shrinks fast. This is a company where macro matters a lot.

Another risk: no room for mistakes. This isn't Franco-Nevada or Wheaton with a dozen cash machines. It's a small royalty shop with a thin margin of safety. One or two assets slipping off schedule, or one operator deciding to pause a mine plan, could hit results hard.

Finally, dilution is always lurking. If the company needs more cash and can't raise debt cheaply, it's likely to tap equity. The convertibles already point to that direction. So if things don't go as planned, shareholders could get diluted just to keep the lights on.

Catalysts

The main catalysts I see is new production starting up. A few key assets are either just beginning to produce or are right on the edge:

  • Borborema just started mining in late Q1 and should ramp to ~83 koz annualized by Q3. GROY has a 2.0% NSR, so that'll show up in revenue soon.

  • Vareš is expected to hit commercial production in Q2. That stream could be one of GROY's better assets, copper-linked and at a 24.5% fixed rate, which adds some commodity diversity too.

  • Côté Gold is hitting design throughput. Full production expected by 2026, but contribution will ramp gradually. GROY gets 0.75% NSR on the southern block.

  • Odyssey continues moving ahead in Quebec. It's a slow build, but GROY's 3.0% NSR on the underground portion (Odyssey North/South) will become valuable once steady-state output kicks in.

If those assets all hit on time, cash flow grows sharply in the second half of 2025 and into 2026. That gives the company a shot not based on hype, but based on actual incoming revenue.

Another potential is the gold price. Spot gold is still hovering, and with conflicts we see emerging in the world, I guess it will stay around $3,300-3,400/oz. GROY's internal estimates use $2,668/oz, so anything above that is upside. Same thing with copper, if it holds near $4.80/lb, the Vareš stream looks even better than modeled. In this market, price trends do the heavy lifting.

Also worth noting: the company continues to add new royalties. They picked up a 1.2% NSR on the Garrison project (Timmins) in March. It's small, but it shows they're still sourcing deals, which matters long term if they want to build a larger platform. And they say they'll be free cash flow positive for full-year 2025. If that actually happens, not adjusted EBITDA, not "on a run-rate basis," but real, after-cost cash flow, that's a clear shift in the story, and then maybe we can look into BUY rating. It would prove they can finally self-fund without more dilution or debt.

Valuation

GROY still looks expensive on most traditional metrics, especially when compared to actual gold producers.

The P/E numbers are not good. FY1 and TTM are non-existent because the company isn't earning anything yet. FY2 shows a P/E of 221, and FY3 drops to 66, which is better, but still very high. Those numbers tell me one thing: the market is pricing in future upside that hasn't materialized yet. So, paying today for royalty streams that might get big in two or three years.

Price/Sales (TTM) sits at 35.38, which is sky-high compared to producers like Caledonia (CMCL), 1.97, or Galiano (GAU), 1.29. Even on EV/Sales (TTM), GROY is at 41.09, way above everyone else in the group. That's the cost of having a wide royalty portfolio that isn't fully online yet, you don't get the cash, but you still carry the market cap.

Price to book is actually not bad, just 0.68, which means the market isn't putting a premium on the asset base itself. But when you look at Price to Cash Flow (TTM), 80.27, it's clear that investors are paying a lot for what little cash is currently being generated.

On EV/EBITDA, it's a similar story. TTM is not meaningful, but the forward EV/EBITDA is around 41, which again is rich, especially compared to CMCL (3.61), GAU (3.27), or even ITRG (3.26).

Until cash flow scales, the stock will likely stay in this middle zone, not cheap enough for value buyers, not fast enough for growth investors.

Summary

Gold Royalty is still early. The portfolio looks solid on paper, Odyssey, Côté, Borborema, and Vareš are all high-quality assets, but most of them haven't fully kicked in yet. Only seven royalties are producing, and even those are still ramping. It's going to take time.

Cash flow turned positive in Q1 2025, which helps. But they're still posting losses and sitting on just over $3 million in cash while carrying more than $50 million in debt. Not a comfortable setup.

There are some real catalysts. Borborema just started producing and should ramp fast. Vareš is close to commercial production. Côté is getting there too. If gold and copper hold near current levels, the second half of 2025 could look very different.

But right now, this is still a wait and see story. HOLD feels right until cash flow becomes consistent and the risk/reward gets a little more balanced.

data.title: Zillow Home Value Index: 'Real' Home Values Hit Lowest Level In Over 4 Years
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Zillow (Z), the real estate listing and brokerage website, provides a wealth of publicly available real estate data. Among these, the Zillow Home Value Index (ZHVI) offers a seasonally adjusted measure of home values and market trends.

Its "flagship" ZHVI is based on data for "all homes, middle tier, smoothed and seasonally adjusted cut," aiming to represent the typical home value rather than the median. Introduced in 2006, the ZHVI includes various segmentations by geography and housing type. Here, we focus on the flagship ZHVI, which reflects typical home values.

Home values fell for a third straight month in May, according to the Zillow Home Value Index. However, after adjusting for inflation, real home values declined for a 13th consecutive month, hitting their lowest level in over four years.

Last month's ZHVI came in at $367,969, down 0.19% from the previous month and up 0.78% from one year ago. However, after adjusting for inflation, the real figures are -0.46% month-over-month and -3.24% year-over-year.

Zillow Home Value Index Methodology

Zillow’s ZHVI is measured using monthly changes in its “Zestimates”, its proprietary home valuation model. The index is meant to be timely, comprehensive, and transparent, published three weeks after the close of a given month. Many other home value indexes are published with a significant time lag - like the S&P at Core Logic Case Shiller Index and the FHFA House Price Index.

ZHVI is calculated with a few assumptions:

  • Monthly changes are calculated with a weighted mean
  • The average Zestimate within a range determines the index level
  • It can be interpreted as the market’s total appreciation for a given geography or cut

In addition, historical appreciation is based on actual historical housing stock versus a fixed basket of homes, and home improvements are considered in appreciation unrelated to market movements.

The first chart below shows the flagship ZHVI series - the “typical home value” since 2000, not adjusted for inflation. The current nominal value is 0.41% off its all-time high from February 2025.

Next, we take a look at the same index values but adjust for inflation using the CPI. As you can see, it’s a different picture. According to the ZHVI, today’s home values are below that of the (inflation-adjusted) housing peak in 2006 and are currently 11.36% off its all-time high from July 2022.

Here is an overlay of the nominal and real indexes for a comparative look.

Housing Prices (ZHVI) and Mortgage Rates

The following charts overlay mortgage rates with both nominal and real ZHVI, highlighting their relationship. Typically, rising interest rates reduce buyer affordability, decreasing demand for homes. This can lead to downward pressure on home prices. With that said, today’s housing market is following a different path because we’re seeing elevated mortgage rates in conjunction with elevated home prices.

Zillow Home Value Index vs. S&P CoreLogic Case-Shiller Index

Zillow claims that the ZHVI is “the most accurate and timely measures of residential real estate prices in the United States" and that its index is more representative of housing stock than the S&P CoreLogic Case-Shiller Home Price Index. Zillow claims this due to its extensive database that includes input from real homeowners as well as their sophisticated home valuation model in the Zestimate.

Zillow believes its ZHVI has distinct advantages over the Case-Shiller index. The Case-Shiller (CS) Index uses the repeat-sales methodology, meaning it measures price changes by looking at homes that have resold in a region, while the ZHVI only includes homes that have sold at least twice in recent history and excludes all new construction.

Here are a few charts pulled directly from a piece by Zillow comparing the two indexes. We tried to get this data to do our own analysis, but the research group there would not provide it. Here’s a link to that article.

© Zillow.com

Another difference between the CS and ZHVI is that CS includes foreclosure resales. Zillow emphasizes that foreclosure resales are “substantially different from non-distressed sales” and are often considerably discounted from such. Zillow claims that using foreclosure resales represents “two very different market segments”. Eric Stubbs, Ph.D. at RBC WM believes foreclosure sales are a matter of taste and says that foreclosures are actual sales, so they do, in fact, affect the price of surrounding properties.

In any case, the ZHVI is another tool in the tool belt to determine home values and is useful due to its timeliness and variety of information.

ETFs associated with residential real estate include: iShares Residential and Multisector Real Estate ETF (REZ).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Pan American Silver: Profit Outlook Surges As Silver Breaks Higher
data.description:
The price of silver has broken to the upside since the beginning of the year, rising by around 26% to over $33 per ounce. For years, investors and analysts, including myself, have viewed silver as an ideal hedge against inflation risk associated with excessively dovish monetary policy. The gold-to-silver ratio has also been at a historical extreme, creating a value opportunity for silver that seems to be closing. Silver also has a relatively high industrial use demand growth, arguably in a growing physical shortage, led by higher conductor demand for electronic and energy systems.

Silver ETFs like (SLV) are a means to invest in silver close to the spot price. However, many investors prefer not to invest in commodity ETFs, instead opting for direct physical possession or investing in silver miners. The largest silver mining stock by market capitalization is Pan American Silver Corp. (NYSE:PAAS) with a market capitalization of $10B. The company will soon grow significantly larger after it completes the acquisition of MAG Silver (MAG), purchasing the stock at a substantial premium of ~25%. Of course, because silver has risen by over 10% since early May, the incomplete acquisition has arguably already paid off.

Pan American is a huge silver company with diversified operations across the Americas. Its purchase of MAG will increase its production in North America and potentially lower its costs per ounce, given MAG's primary mine has an all-in sustaining cost of just $12 per equivalent ounce sold, with around 19 million ounces in production. Pan American's 2024 AISC was ~$19 with 21 MOZ production and a staggering 468M in proven and probable reserves. PAAS also pays a dividend of ~$0.40 per share, a low 1.4% yield, but more than many mining stocks.

Given the acquisition and the sharp rise in silver, investors are likely curious if PAAS may soon rise to a new all-time high. Indeed, a significant silver breakout should benefit large silver miners, such as PAAS, more than silver investment funds. That said, despite its vast size, PAAS has underperformed the underlying commodity for some time, potentially discouraging investment despite its potential for profit growth.

A Look At Silver Mining Cost Inflation

The fundamental issue I've seen in both silver miners and gold is their exposure to cost growth. Silver and gold mining are extremely energy and labor-cost-intensive because mine yields are relatively low. A great deal of ore must be extracted and processed for a small amount of precious metals. Silver and gold are fundamentally hedges against inflation, but if inflation pushes labor, fuel, and capital costs up faster, their incomes will fall.

Pan American's operating margin is strongly correlated with the price of silver; however, a higher silver price has been necessary for its margins to be positive. Its margins are elevated today, but are not significantly higher than they've been over the past decade, despite silver reaching decade highs in the most recent consecutive quarters. See below:

In 2019, Pan American Silver reported a silver segment AISC of around $11. Today, it is 72% higher at about $19. The consumer price index has risen by 27% since early 2019, while the price of silver has increased by about 120%. Thus, the net change is an increase in profits, but only on the ~28% net rise in silver relative to the increase in its AISC, which is growing far faster than inflation at around 10% per year since 2019.

Although PAAS is approximately 64% more volatile than the silver ETF (SLV), its beta to SLV is around 1.1, given its correlation of about 0.68. PAAS outperformed SLV sharply in 2020 when silver prices initially spiked during the Fed's considerable quantitative easing effort. However, as global central bank easing efforts exacerbated supply-shock inflation, silver production costs rose similarly, pushing PAAS's relative performance back down. Overall, PAAS has performed about the same as SLV and the silver miner ETF (SIL) since before 2020:

Notably, the peaks at troughs in PAAS's relative performance to SLV generally align with those of the relative performance of silver to energy commodities. Silver miners are not a hedge against inflation because, in general, we can expect inflation to push the price of all commodities higher. Since silver mining is primarily energy-intensive, silver miners' margins can be associated with the relative price of silver to fuel inputs. Interestingly, the silver-to-energy price ratio is elevated again today:

The elevation in this index points to higher profit margins for PAAS in the immediate future. Indeed, Pan American reported an AISC far below guidance and expected levels in Q1 at just $13.94 for its silver segment. Fuel prices were historically low in Q1, with crude falling to around its lowest level since the pandemic lockdown crash. Notably, labor costs in key production areas, such as Mexico, have declined. Mexico and most Latin American nations have experienced relatively high labor cost growth in recent years, despite having extremely low unemployment and decent GDP growth. Mexico's business confidence trend is increasingly bearish, indicating a slowing economy that may result in less labor cost inflation for Pan American. This trend is also reflected in Argentina's economy, which has come down from the extreme inflation of years past.

PAAS Profit Outlook and Valuation

The company's immediate profit outlook is robust compared to TTM levels due to the combined decrease in cost factors and rises in silver and gold. Since its acquisition is not yet complete, I will project based only on Pan American's metrics, but note that the MAG deal is attractive mainly due to silver price appreciation since the deal was priced.

At the beginning of the year, Pan American looked to silver production of around 20.5M ounces and gold of around 770K ounces. Its AISC forecast was $16.25 to $18.25, with gold's cost forecasted at $1.52K to $1.63K per ounce. Its Q1 performance generally beat cost expectations with lower AISCs. Though oil is higher today, there is a general lag between energy costs and production costs, so for now, I'll assume costs remain at the low end of the projected range.

Annualizing, Pan American's expected silver revenue for the next year is likely around $745M (current price times production guidance), while gold is higher at $2.6B. The company's gold output has increased, making it less of a pure-play silver company, but one that benefits greatly from the ongoing gold bull market.

At today's market prices and the low-end of Pan American's cost guidance, we can expect it to generate around $20 per ounce of operating profit per silver ounce mined and ~$1.86K per ounce of gold. The net segment operating income should be around $410M for silver and ~$1.43B from gold, or $1.84B combined.

AISC figures do not include depreciation, growth CapEx, exploration, financing cost, income taxes, and one-off cost or gain factors. Pan American's TTM operating income is $530M, but its cash from operations is higher at $837M due to the impact of depreciation. The company also had $54M in net interest costs. Accounting for interest and taxes, I estimate Pan American's net operating cash flow (adjusted for sustaining CapEx) at around $1.25B, well-above its TTM CFO of ~$840M.

Thus, its forward price-to-cash flow may be as low as 8.4 times, potentially leaving it undervalued. Of course, my assumption is based on precious metals sustaining their recent gains while costs remain at the low end of the expected range. The recent rise in oil prices associated with the Israel-Iran conflict could undermine that, potentially dramatically if oil prices rapidly rise over $100, as I suspect.

The Bottom Line

Arguably, the prices of silver and gold are rising in anticipation of monetary distress in the US and other key banking countries. Extremely high debt burdens, higher borrowing costs, and legislation that increases deficits accelerate a potential government financing crisis. For years, I've maintained the view that the US cannot ease its debt burden without having sustained high inflation. Furthermore, Trump's efforts to encourage the Federal Reserve to ease monetary policy may exacerbate inflation in the immediate future.

Gold and, to a lesser extent, silver are expensive compared to interest rates after expected inflation. The silver and gold rally may be a bubble, or it may be a fundamental sign of an emerging inflationary debt crisis. The risk of an oil price shock associated with Iran could exacerbate this issue, mirroring the inflationary wave of the 1980s following the Iranian oil crisis. If so, the Federal Reserve's ability to manage higher inflation and the economy will be compromised, as the Fed is unable to reduce the impact of supply-side inflation without harming economic demand.

If we look at immediate figures, PAAS is potentially undervalued. The company is poised to generate excellent cash flows, given that precious metals prices are rising while AISCs are not, a trend that contrasts with the experience over the past five years. However, in the long run, there is no fundamental reason why costs will not rise proportionally to inflation. I am bullish on PAAS due to the rise in silver and gold, and it may be a good short-term investment. However, I prefer SLV and GLD if we assume an investment horizon greater than a year because I expect energy costs to rise as much, and potentially more than, the price of precious metals. I think PAAS is a decent bet today, but its most significant risk may be a large increase in energy costs should my concerns regarding the oil market become a reality.

data.title: Martin Midstream Partners: Decent Q1 Results, But Continued Debt Reduction Needed
data.description:
Martin Midstream Partners (NASDAQ:MMLP) reported decent Q1 2025 results and maintained its full-year guidance. I am also modestly more optimistic about the overall economic environment compared to a couple of months ago, so I am increasing my estimate of Martin's value slightly from $3.25 per unit to $3.35 per unit.

With Martin trading at $3.12 per unit and offering a minimal $0.005 per unit quarterly distribution, I remain neutral on it. Martin appears unlikely to increase its distribution in the near future, as debt reduction remains a main priority.

Martin is a K-1 issuer, so that reduces investor interest, especially when combined with Martin's sub-1% current distribution yield.

Q1 2025 Results

Martin reported $27.8 million in adjusted EBITDA in Q1 2025, down from $30.4 million in Q1 2024. It reaffirmed its full-year guidance for $109.1 million in adjusted EBITDA though and is 25% of the way to that number after Q1 2025.

Martin noted that its Sulfur Services segment had increased sales volumes due to customers ordering ahead of an anticipated Q2 2025 price increase. As a result, Q2 2025 may be a bit weaker due to that demand being pulled forward. Martin's Sulfur Services segment delivered $4.8 million higher adjusted EBITDA compared to Q1 2024, with $0.9 million of that due to contributions from its electronic level sulfuric acid plant (that started contributing in Q4 2024).

Martin also mentioned that its Transportation segment had increased demand in Q1 2025 compared to Q4 2024, although land transportation rates saw some pressure. Martin's Transportation segment generated a $1.5 million increase in adjusted EBITDA compared to Q4 2024, although this was down by $5.2 million compared to Q1 2024.

Martin reported $9.1 million in distributable cash flow and $8.2 million in adjusted free cash flow for the quarter. This is 33% and 44% of its full-year targets for distributable cash flow and adjusted free cash flow, respectively. Martin expects increased capex and plant turnaround costs in future quarters, so that will reduce its free cash flow, even though adjusted EBITDA is expected to average near Q1 levels during the rest of the year.

Notes On Full-Year Results

I previously had noted that the uncertain economic outlook presented some downside risks to Martin's guidance. As a result, my base case scenario was that Martin would fall around 4% short of its full-year adjusted EBITDA guidance.

I am now modestly more optimistic about things. The trade war has de-escalated somewhat, and there appears to be less risk of a near-term US recession now.

Thus, I am now assuming that Martin will end up with around $108 million in 2025 adjusted EBITDA now, about 1% lower than its guidance.

Martin's 11.5% secured notes due 2028 have also rebounded a bit and now trade with a 8.2% yield-to-maturity. This is down from slightly over 10% in early April, and close to the 7.6% yield-to-maturity in late March 2025.

Leverage And Debt

While Martin reported $8.2 million in adjusted free cash flow in Q1 2025, its outstanding debt went up by $12.5 million. This was largely due to the $23 million interest payment on its secured notes due 2028. Those interest payments are in Q1 and Q3.

Martin may end up with around $421 million in net debt at the end of 2025 now. This would result in its adjusted leverage ratio being approximately 3.9x, while its interest coverage ratio should be around 2.1x. Martin's first-lien leverage ratio should be around 0.3x to 0.4x.

Thus Martin should remain in compliance with its credit facility covenants. I do not expect Martin to increase its quarterly distribution from $0.005 per unit for the time being due to its need to further reduce its debt and then deal with its 2028 notes.

Estimated Valuation

I've slightly increased my estimate of Martin's value from $3.25 per unit to $3.35 per unit to reflect the modest improvement in my near-term outlook for it. I continue to assume that Martin can average $110 million in adjusted EBITDA over a longer period of time.

It will probably be at least a couple of years before Martin increases its distribution. A scenario where Martin can reduce its leverage to 3.5x or less and refinances its 2028 notes at a lower (such as 9%) interest rate would probably be conducive to an increased distribution.

Investor interest will likely be limited until then by Martin being a K-1 issuer with a minimal distribution.

Conclusion

Martin Midstream's Q1 2025 results were decent and there appears to be less risk of a near-term recession now. I've thus increased my estimate around its full-year adjusted EBITDA by $3 million and increased my estimate of its value by $0.10 per unit to $3.35 per unit.

Martin's main task will be to work on reducing its debt and get itself into position to refinance its 11.5% secured notes due 2028 at a lower interest rate.

If it can pay off its credit facility debt and refinance its secured notes at a 9% interest rate, it would save around $15 million per year in interest costs. This would make a substantial difference to its free cash flow, given that it is guiding to around $19 million in adjusted free cash flow currently.

The interest savings would give it more room to offer a decent distribution, which is probably necessary for Martin to gain more investor interest. If it put that interest savings towards the distribution, it could offer a $0.10 per unit quarterly distribution.

data.title: Kiwetinohk Energy: Still Focused On Upstream
data.description:
Kiwetinohk Energy (OTCPK:KWTEF) management announced "strategic alternatives". Sometimes that can freeze a stock price for a while until the idea behind that announcement is known by the market. It usually happens because there is a belief that the value of the company is not being realized by the market. With the current threat of a trade war and tariffs along with a potential worldwide recession (or worse) it should be no surprise that the oil and gas business is out-of-favor. Now, whether the process announced will unlock that value that management believes is there is up for discussion. In any event, you have a very experienced founder at the helm who is very much on the side of shareholders.

Last Article

The last article noted that the company was largely focused on upstream. Much of the other stuff envisioned was relegated to the planning stage, complete with delays.

But again, the upstream business was doing fine. It was just the value of the whole business as it was at the time that was a concern. Now it would appear that management is losing some patience with the situation.

But Mr. Market is not an entity that can be rushed. This is especially true with new ideas. Ever since unconventional stopped growing rapidly in the United States, one would think that the oil and gas business is "on its last legs" from the relative valuation of these companies compared to other parts of the market.

But nothing could be further from the truth. Oil and gas will likely be needed long after we are gone. That likely means that the business will cycle back into favor eventually, as it has in the past.

It may also be true that some of the backers want to get out of this investment. The company may be looking for a way to get them out without a major disturbance to the stock price or company prospects.

Financial Results

The increase of oil and condensate in the production mix had a favorable impact on the average price received by the company.

(Kiwetinohk Energy is a Canadian company that reports using Canadian dollars unless otherwise noted.)

Also notice that as the production volume has climbed, the company has managed to reduce operating costs. Now, transportation expenses will vary from one producer to another. About all that can be assumed there is that management weighs the benefits of more transportation expense against the price received when possible.

Cash flow climbed nicely due to the rise in natural gas prices as well as the increasingly favorable production mix. From an adjusted funds flow viewpoint, this stock could prove to be very cheap. What is needed here is a track record of that kind of funds flow for a few years. But it will not take any extraordinary moves by management to do that. That may make this stock a sleeper.

Note that the current quarterly cash flow is about one-half the net debt figure. That means that the company has a very conservative balance sheet.

History And Outlook

This company has had negative cash flow in order to get production to a decent level for cost optimization. Fortunately, in Canada, the debt market allowed this. That is a big difference between the United States debt market and the Canadian debt market.

The operational efficiencies gained are allowing management to hold capital costs constant while still growing production. The result is that the company will have market desired free funds flow in the current fiscal year.

Depending upon drilling results (and aging production results as well), if management can continue to increase the percentage of more valuable liquids in the production mix, then free cash flow could experience some rather rapid growth.

Note that the latest wells have a considerably higher percentage of liquids than is the existing production mix. This bodes very well for the growth of future profitability at a rate in excess of the production growth rate.

However, it is likely that debt reduction will be the objective as long as the longer-term products remain delayed. That will give the company more financial flexibility when the time arrives for those projects to actually move to construction and operations.

Realized Natural Gas Prices

This company realizes some of the best natural gas prices in the Canadian industry. Very few companies get their production to strong markets. Instead, many managements "dump" their production to the nearest transportation entity. That often leaves production to be sold in a relatively nearby, saturated market.

It takes some planning to consistently get production to a stronger market. This is one of those managements that does exactly that.

Such strategies do not work 100% of the time. But a good strategy will far outweigh those disappointing years.

Summary

This company is off to a good start. Canada has a fair amount of acreage that is now cost competitive thanks to technology improvements. Much of the area where the company is active was known for its dry gas production. Technology advances have now made the liquids rich interval cost competitive to produce. As a result, there are areas in Western Canada experiencing an industry revival.

The strategic alternatives announcement may "freeze" the company stock until more concrete details emerge. However, I would suspect that this company will not be sold unless a much more friendly industry atmosphere emerges. Just remember though that anything is possible.

As part of a basket, this remains a strong buy idea. Pat Carlson, who heads this company, has a lot of experience building and selling companies. That gives this relatively new company an advantage over a lot of new companies in the industry.

Risks

Any relatively new business is an above-average risk. That is definitely the case here.

Pat Carlson, who effectively runs this company as a founder (among his other titles) has an unusual amount of experience at building and selling companies. The loss of his services could prove a critical matter to the company.

The article was written with the assumption that the current talk of tariffs and trade wars would be replaced with something permanent and constructive.

Right now, the business really consists of upstream operations. The other business ideas envisioned for this company may never happen.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SPYD Delivers Stability In Unstable Times
data.description:
The SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 High Dividend ETF (NYSEARCA:SPYD) tracks the S&P 500 High Dividend Index. The selection criteria are a ranking of announced indicated dividend for the next year divided by the share price at rebalancing. That is, the expected dividend yield for the next 12 months. The index and SPYD have 80 constituents. 64 of those are selected as described. The remaining is picked from existing holdings in rank order if they appear within the 96 top yielding stocks at the time of rebalancing. If seats still exist, then names beyond 64 are picked again on dividend yield merit. That is, the selection is mostly based on yield, but a preference to existing names (within a strict yield criteria). The index is equal weighted at each quarterly rebalance. An interesting and simple criteria and weighting methodology. Aims to pick on the basis of yield rather than other growth or stability variables of the companies. Expect higher than average yields compared to dividend focused ETFs also relying on growth variables (like say VIG).

Dividend Yield and NAV Growth

The Yield is excellent and averages above 4% over time. Stable and reliable. Looking at it from the lens of an income plan, the NAV does not depreciate or erode too much over time, as defensive and value names mostly make the cut. A slightly growing NAV means the yield grows over time and a growing base, in absolute quantum. A good bet for an income oriented outlook (provided drawdowns are manageable, which we will explore later). Not for growth purposes.

Portfolio Characteristics

Analyzing the top 10 is skipped as the ETF is equal weighted. Sectoral allocations are interesting. Property Trusts and Real estate bets are the major sectoral leaders. Followed by Utilities. Consumer Defensives and Financials also have material weights. Technology is notably missing.

High yields of REITs and real estate exposure align with the core promise of SPYD. The emphasis on real estate will rely on lower interest rate sooner than later to outperform. For now, the yields look generally stable and lucrative. Utilities are likely to do well in this late cycle and rate cut environment and gives a decent defensive flavor to the holdings. Consumer defensives cushion slowdowns. Financials will rely on macro growth, cooling inflation and favorable rate regimes. The sectoral tilt is a good mix of defensives that will likely do well across macro situations taken together, with cyclicals and defensive weightings helping cushion downside risk in downturns. The concentration risk is also notably lower, both at individual stock level and sectoral level. No technology is the final nail in the growth path. So you know what you are in for - stable growth across macros. Defensive income generating vehicle.

Performance Comparisons

A comparison with VYM shows that SPYD's equal weighted pure yield filter works better than VYM's more quality filters on dividends and an emphasis on large caps. This comparison is on a total return perspective. Yields for SPYD are higher. Even drawdowns are not worse off.

Comparison with a stronger total return potential like VIG. VIG has a lower yield and focusses on picking stronger companies with dividend growth as a criteria (not yield alone). Yet does not provide either greater outperformance nor limited drawdowns in comparison.

DHS does not restrict the universe to quality, top names like SPYD. Here the emphasis is dividend yield without restricting the universe. Meaning, it should be expected to provide riskier and higher yields over time. Yet SPYD again outperforms in total returns and significantly so. Yes, the drawdowns are higher for a similar yield income periodically.

And finally, a comparison with SPY to show how you get better stability at the cost of growth. While the long term return potential is significant, remember the last decade has seen an overall bull run with short (but deep) crashes in the interim. Picking SPYD is not based on long-term results so far, but on risk management principles.

A look at drawdowns during the 2022-23 rate hike pressures is a textbook example of how SPYD reduced drawdowns. For a considerable amount of time, SPYD kept its head above water, even as SPY even corrected by ~20%. And the SPYD is not a one-trick pony for rate hike cycles alone. The sectoral allocation, as discussed before, is versatile enough to weather slowdowns, wider crashes and geopolitical drags with varying degrees of outperformance.

SPYD is a Buy

SPYD qualifies in several use cases. And I believe the core of its versatility is REIT inclusion that several ETFs don't allow. SPYD performs as a value ETF, dividend yield ETF, income ETF and defensive ETF all at once. The sectoral holdings and yield show that mix as well. Since, significant role in SPYD's Buy case rests on REITs, investors may alternatively consider loading up in REITs alone. However, that will mean reducing the benefit of defensives, financials and other sectors in SPYD that cushion higher rates pressure on REITs.

SPYD's case is further strengthened in the current scenario, as I have been advocating trimming some growth exposure as we brace for slowdown and uncertainties. Some of the trimmed capital should flow into SPYD. SPYD is a core holding otherwise too. Buy SPYD tactically for the next few quarters, as well as the longer-term stabilizer in your portfolio. Particularly if income is an important component of your objective.

data.title: Evaluating The 6.3% Bond Yield Of Southern Company
data.description:
Many income-oriented investors have a stake in the common stock of Southern Company (NYSE:SO) for its reliable dividend. The utility has not cut its dividend for 79 consecutive years, it has raised its dividend for 24 consecutive years (including this year’s raise) and is offering a dividend yield of 3.4%. Given also its resilience to recessions and its reliable growth trajectory thanks to the regulated nature of its business, Southern is a popular stock in the income-oriented investment community.

What most of these investors do not know is the fact that Southern offers a much higher yield (6.3%) via its recently issued bond. A few months ago, the company issued 60-year junior subordinated notes (NYSE:SOJF), which have a 6.5% nominal yield. As these bonds are currently trading 3% above their par value of $25, they are offering a 6.3% yield to maturity. A 6.3% yield with a wide margin of safety, from one of the most defensive companies in the investing universe, is certainly appealing to many income-oriented investors. In this article, we will evaluate the 60-year bond of Southern.

Risk #1: The issuer

Before buying a bond, investors should assess the risk of the issuer, i.e., the risk of a default of the company. Fortunately, in this case, the issuer is one of the most defensive companies. Southern is a state-regulated utility that engages in the generation, transmission and distribution of electricity. The company generates electricity via various forms of energy, such as nuclear, coal, hydroelectric, solar and wind, and serves approximately 9 million customers.

Thanks to the state-regulated nature of its business and the immense capital expenses required to enter this business, which form high barriers to potential new entrants, Southern has always enjoyed a wide business moat and has proved one of the most resilient companies to recessions and other downturns. In the Great Recession, the worst financial crisis of the past 80 years, Southern saw its earnings per share edge down only 1%. In 2020, which was marked by the severe recession caused by the pandemic, Southern grew its adjusted earnings per share 5%, to a new all-time high. In 2022, when inflation skyrocketed to a 40-year high of 9.1%, many companies were severely hurt due to high cost inflation and high interest expense, but Southern continued growing its earnings per share to a new all-time high, as it was able to pass its incremental costs to its customers. To cut a long story short, thanks to its dominant business position, Southern has proved resilient to all kinds of downturns throughout its 79-year history.

Southern has also exhibited a remarkably consistent performance record. The utility has grown its adjusted earnings per share in 8 of the last nine years, at a 4.0% average annual rate. It has also provided guidance for earnings per share of $4.20-$4.30 this year. At the mid-point, this guidance corresponds to 5% growth over the prior year. Southern has missed the analysts’ consensus only three times in the last 33 quarters. Given this exceptional record of beating estimates and the tendency of management to issue cautious guidance, one can rest assured that the company will keep growing its earnings per share this year. Overall, the consistent and reliable growth trajectory of Southern is a testament to its defensive business model.

Moreover, Southern has a decent balance sheet for a utility. As a side note, utilities tend to carry a higher amount of debt than other companies thanks to their reliable cash flows. Southern has net debt (as per Buffett’s formula: Net debt = total liabilities – cash – receivables) of $104.8 billion. This amount is 108% of the market capitalization of the stock and approximately 22 times the earnings of the company in the last 12 months and hence it is not negligible. Nevertheless, Southern has a solid interest coverage ratio of 2.9x. Given also its resilience to downturns and its reliable growth trajectory, the company is not likely to have any problem servicing its debt.

Even in the extremely unlikely scenario of a potential liquidity problem, Southern will have ample room to preserve funds by cutting its dividend. The coupon payments of the bond cannot be suspended unless the dividend is eliminated and the company declares bankruptcy. The dividend of Southern amounts to $3.0 billion per year, corresponding to a payout ratio of 68%. This payout ratio is reasonable for a utility. It is also important to keep in mind that Southern has never cut its dividend throughout its 79-year history and has raised its dividend for 24 consecutive years. Therefore, the dividend of Southern has a wide margin of safety. Even in an extremely adverse scenario, Southern will have ample room to preserve funds by cutting its dividend. Therefore, the 6.3% bond yield of Southern should be considered essentially safe.

Risk #2: Interest rates

The only material risk of the 6.3% bond of Southern is the future path of interest rates. Just like all the fixed-income securities, bonds are highly sensitive to interest rates. If interest rates increase, they will render the bond of Southern less attractive, and hence they are likely to exert pressure on the price of the bond.

However, the Fed is unlikely to raise interest rates. It had begun in September to cut interest rates off their 23-year highs but then it paused its interest rate cutting cycle due to the announcement of excessive tariffs by the new U.S. government. Excessive tariffs could cause a rebound of inflation due to an increased cost of imported goods. Therefore, it was a prudent move of the Fed to wait and see whether the U.S. government would actually impose high tariffs on the trading partners of the country.

Fortunately, the U.S. government has markedly softened its stance in the last two months due to the sell-off of U.S. bonds that took place after the “Liberation Day”. The U.S. 10-year bond yield is currently standing at a nearly 17-year high of 4.40%. Due to a multi-decade high debt-to-GDP ratio (124%) and a high budget deficit (-6.2% in each of the last two years), the interest expense of the U.S. government has skyrocketed to an all-time high.

Source: U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis

If the U.S. 10-year bond yield remains high or increases further, it will greatly burden the budget of the government and will fully or partly offset the cost-cutting efforts of the government. As a result, the government has drastically softened its stance on tariffs, in an effort to calm the bond market.

Moreover, due to the aforementioned high debt and budget deficit and the resultant recent downgrade of the credit rating of the U.S. by Moody’s from Aaa to Aa1, the government will probably have to reduce spending and raise some taxes at some point. When that happens, it will probably exert a drag on economic growth. According to the latest forecast of analysts, U.S. economic growth is expected to decelerate from 2.8% in 2024 to 1.4% this year. As a result, the Fed has good chances of resuming its interest rate cutting cycle at some point in 2025 or 2026. Lower interest rates will provide a tailwind to the price of the bond of Southern; not only bondholders will keep receiving a 6.3% yield-on-cost, but they will also benefit from the appreciation of the price of the bond.

Risk #3: Early call

Southern has the right to redeem its bond from 3/15/2030 at its par value of $25. If interest rates are much lower in five years from now, the company may choose to call its bond and issue another bond, with a lower interest rate. Nevertheless, even this adverse scenario is benign for bondholders. Even if Southern calls its bond on the earliest possible date, on 3/15/2030, bondholders will receive a 5.8% yield until that date.

Comparison to benchmark yields and the stock of Southern

It is worth comparing the 6.3% yield of the 60-year bond of Southern to the yields of U.S. bonds. The 30-year U.S. bond yield and the 10-year U.S. bond yield are currently standing at 4.89% and 4.40%, respectively. Of course, U.S. bonds are safer than corporate bonds but, as mentioned above, the risk of a default of Southern is essentially negligible. Therefore, the 6.3% bond yield of Southern appears fairly attractive when compared to U.S. bond yields.

It is also worth examining whether the bond or the stock of Southern is more attractive. The stock is offering a 3.4% dividend yield, which is much lower than the 6.3% bond yield of the utility. On the other hand, the company has grown its earnings per share by 4.0% per year on average over the last nine years. It has also provided guidance for 5%-7% growth of earnings per share over the long run. Analysts seem to agree with this guidance, as they expect 6% average annual growth of earnings per share over the next four years. Therefore, the stock of Southern offers a lower yield but decent growth prospects.

Overall, the yield of the bond of Southern is nearly twice as much as the dividend yield of the stock, but the latter offers the potential for mid-single-digit annual capital appreciation. Therefore, it is up to the temperament of an investor to choose between the bond and the stock of Southern. The 6.3% bond yield should appear highly attractive to income-oriented investors, whereas the stock of Southern should appear more attractive to growth-oriented investors.

Final thoughts

The stock of Southern is popular in the income-oriented investing community for its above-average yield, its reliable dividend growth and its proven resilience to recessions. Most investors do not know that the utility also offers a 6.3% bond yield, which is nearly double the dividend yield of the stock. The 6.3% bond yield should be appealing to many income-oriented investors, particularly to those who expect lower interest rates in the upcoming years.
